content.text: Why Trunk Stability Requires Feature Flags for AI Features

Trunk-based development works when every commit to main is deployable. For conventional code, this is achievable: a commit either compiles or it doesn't; a function either returns the right value or it doesn't. For AI features, deployability is harder to verify — a prompt change that passes the eval suite in CI may still cause unexpected behaviour for a subset of real users in production.

Feature flags decouple deployment from activation. The prompt change is on trunk the moment it is merged; whether users see it is a separate decision controlled by an environment variable, a config value, or a percentage-based rollout rule.

This sub-subtopic defines how to use feature flags to keep trunk stable for AI features: flag types, state machine from merge to general availability, the eval and SLO gates that govern each transition, and the code patterns that make flag logic testable.

attrs.alt: Feature flag state machine: Merged (flag off) → Internal Testing → Canary (1-5%) → General Availability. Rollback paths go back to Merged. Eval gate governs Internal→Canary; SLO gate governs Canary→GA.

content.text: The diagram shows four states: Merged (flag off), Internal Testing, Canary (1-5% of users), and General Availability. Normal progression moves left to right. Two rollback paths (shown as dashed red arrows) allow immediate retreat to the Merged state if problems are detected. The Eval Gate controls whether Internal Testing may advance to Canary; the SLO Gate controls whether Canary may advance to GA.
Not all feature flags for AI features serve the same purpose. Using the right flag type for each situation makes the codebase easier to reason about.
A changed system prompt or new prompt variant
A new or modified tool schema available to Claude
A model version upgrade (e.g. claude-3-5 → claude-4)
The fraction of users who see a feature
Emergency disable of the whole AI feature

Kill switches deserve special attention: they should be implemented as the outermost check, before any other flag evaluation, so that a single environment variable immediately disables the entire AI path regardless of rollout percentage or other flag state.

A simple, testable feature flag implementation uses environment variables as the source of truth. Flags are evaluated once at request time, not cached across requests, so an operator can change a flag without redeploying.

import anthropic, os, hashlib
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class FeatureFlagContext:
    """All feature flag state for one request."""
    disable_ai_triage: bool = False
    prompt_escalation_v2: bool = False
    tool_triage_v2: bool = False
    model_upgrade: bool = False
    rollout_pct_escalation: int = 0

def read_flags(user_id: str = "") -> FeatureFlagContext:
    """Read feature flags from environment; apply rollout percentage if user_id given."""
    pct = int(os.environ.get("ROLLOUT_PCT_ESCALATION", "0"))

    # Rollout: deterministic per user_id so the same user always gets the same experience
    user_bucket = int(hashlib.md5(user_id.encode()).hexdigest(), 16) % 100 if user_id else 0
    in_rollout = user_bucket < pct

    return FeatureFlagContext(
        disable_ai_triage=os.environ.get("FF_DISABLE_AI_TRIAGE", "false") == "true",
        prompt_escalation_v2=(
            os.environ.get("FF_ESCALATION_V2", "false") == "true"
            or in_rollout
        ),
        tool_triage_v2=os.environ.get("FF_TRIAGE_TOOL_V2", "false") == "true",
        model_upgrade=os.environ.get("FF_MODEL_UPGRADE", "false") == "true",
        rollout_pct_escalation=pct,
    )

def get_system_prompt(flags: FeatureFlagContext) -> str:
    base = """You are a customer support assistant. Help users resolve issues accurately."""

    if flags.prompt_escalation_v2:
        base += """

When your confidence that you can resolve the issue is below 0.6, escalate to a human specialist.
Say: "I want to make sure you get the best help possible. Let me connect you with a specialist." """

    return base

def get_model(flags: FeatureFlagContext) -> str:
    if flags.model_upgrade:
        return "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001"
    return "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001"  # Replace with previous model when upgrading

def handle_request(user_id: str, user_message: str) -> str:
    flags = read_flags(user_id)

    # Kill switch: outermost check
    if flags.disable_ai_triage:
        return "Our AI assistant is temporarily unavailable. Please contact support@example.com."

    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    system_prompt = get_system_prompt(flags)
    model = get_model(flags)

    response = client.messages.create(
        model=model,
        max_tokens=512,
        system=system_prompt,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_message}]
    )
    return response.content[0].text

Feature flags must be testable in isolation. If flag evaluation reads from os.environ directly inside the function under test, tests become fragile and order-dependent. Instead, pass the flag context as a parameter so tests can supply any flag state.

import unittest
from unittest.mock import patch

# --- Code under test ---
def get_system_prompt(flags: FeatureFlagContext) -> str:
    base = "You are a customer support assistant."
    if flags.prompt_escalation_v2:
        base += " Escalate if confidence < 0.6."
    return base

# --- Tests ---
class TestFeatureFlags(unittest.TestCase):

    def test_base_prompt_when_flag_off(self):
        flags = FeatureFlagContext(prompt_escalation_v2=False)
        prompt = get_system_prompt(flags)
        self.assertNotIn("Escalate", prompt)

    def test_escalation_added_when_flag_on(self):
        flags = FeatureFlagContext(prompt_escalation_v2=True)
        prompt = get_system_prompt(flags)
        self.assertIn("Escalate", prompt)

    def test_kill_switch_bypasses_all_flags(self):
        flags = FeatureFlagContext(
            disable_ai_triage=True,
            prompt_escalation_v2=True,  # Would normally add escalation
        )
        # handle_request should return fallback without calling Claude
        with patch("anthropic.Anthropic") as mock_client:
            result = handle_request("user-123", "Help me", flags=flags)
        mock_client.assert_not_called()
        self.assertIn("temporarily unavailable", result)

    def test_rollout_bucket_is_deterministic(self):
        # Same user_id always gets same bucket
        pct = 20
        with patch.dict(os.environ, {"ROLLOUT_PCT_ESCALATION": str(pct)}):
            flags_a = read_flags("user-abc")
            flags_b = read_flags("user-abc")
        self.assertEqual(flags_a.prompt_escalation_v2, flags_b.prompt_escalation_v2)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    unittest.main()
Eval Gate: Internal Testing to Canary
Before advancing a feature from internal testing to a 1-5% canary rollout, the eval suite must pass completely. This gate runs in CI when the rollout percentage environment variable changes.

import anthropic, json, sys
from pathlib import Path

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def run_eval_gate(spec_path: str, flags: FeatureFlagContext) -> dict:
    """Run the full eval suite with the given flag state. Returns pass/fail summary."""
    spec = json.loads(Path(spec_path).read_text())
    system_prompt = get_system_prompt(flags)
    model = get_model(flags)

    results = []
    for case in spec["eval_cases"]:
        response = client.messages.create(
            model=model,
            max_tokens=512,
            system=system_prompt,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": case["input"]}]
        )
        output = response.content[0].text

        violations = [
            rule for rule in case.get("must_not_do", [])
            if rule.lower() in output.lower()
        ]
        must_do_failures = [
            signal for signal in case.get("must_do_signals", [])
            if signal.lower() not in output.lower()
        ]

        results.append({
            "case_id": case["id"],
            "passed": not violations and not must_do_failures,
            "violations": violations,
            "must_do_failures": must_do_failures,
        })

    total = len(results)
    failed = sum(1 for r in results if not r["passed"])
    return {
        "total": total,
        "passed": total - failed,
        "failed": failed,
        "results": results,
        "gate_passed": failed == 0,
    }

if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Run with escalation_v2 flag enabled to test the new prompt path
    flags = FeatureFlagContext(prompt_escalation_v2=True)
    summary = run_eval_gate("specs/behaviour_spec.json", flags)

    print(f"Eval gate: {summary['passed']}/{summary['total']} passed")
    for r in summary["results"]:
        if not r["passed"]:
            print(f"  FAIL {r['case_id']}: violations={r['violations']}, must_do={r['must_do_failures']}")

    sys.exit(0 if summary["gate_passed"] else 1)
SLO Gate: Canary to General Availability

After a canary runs for at least 24 hours with SLO metrics stable, the feature may advance to general availability. This gate is checked by an operator script that queries your observability platform.

import anthropic, statistics
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class CanaryMetrics:
    error_rate_pct: float       # Fraction of requests returning 5xx or timeout
    p99_latency_ms: float       # 99th percentile latency
    escalation_rate_change: float  # Change in human escalation rate vs baseline (positive = more escalations)
    hours_stable: float         # Hours since last metric anomaly

# SLO thresholds — set these based on your product requirements
SLO = {
    "max_error_rate_pct": 1.0,          # < 1% errors
    "max_p99_latency_ms": 4000,         # < 4 seconds at p99
    "max_escalation_rate_change": 0.05, # < 5 percentage point increase in escalations
    "min_hours_stable": 24,             # At least 24 hours of stability
}

def check_slo_gate(metrics: CanaryMetrics) -> dict:
    """Evaluate whether the canary meets all SLO thresholds for promotion to GA."""
    checks = {
        "error_rate": metrics.error_rate_pct <= SLO["max_error_rate_pct"],
        "p99_latency": metrics.p99_latency_ms <= SLO["max_p99_latency_ms"],
        "escalation_rate": metrics.escalation_rate_change <= SLO["max_escalation_rate_change"],
        "stability_window": metrics.hours_stable >= SLO["min_hours_stable"],
    }
    all_passed = all(checks.values())

    return {
        "gate_passed": all_passed,
        "checks": checks,
        "metrics": {
            "error_rate_pct": metrics.error_rate_pct,
            "p99_latency_ms": metrics.p99_latency_ms,
            "escalation_rate_change": metrics.escalation_rate_change,
            "hours_stable": metrics.hours_stable,
        },
        "recommendation": "Promote to GA" if all_passed else "Hold or rollback canary",
    }

# Example usage
def promote_canary_check():
    # In production: fetch these from your observability platform (Datadog, Prometheus, etc.)
    current_metrics = CanaryMetrics(
        error_rate_pct=0.3,
        p99_latency_ms=2100,
        escalation_rate_change=0.02,
        hours_stable=26,
    )

    result = check_slo_gate(current_metrics)
    print(f"SLO gate: {'PASS' if result['gate_passed'] else 'FAIL'}")
    for check_name, passed in result["checks"].items():
        status = "OK" if passed else "FAIL"
        print(f"  {status}: {check_name}")
    print(f"  Recommendation: {result['recommendation']}")
    return result
Flag Cleanup: Removing Flags After GA

Feature flags accumulate technical debt. Every flag that is not removed after the feature reaches GA adds a conditional branch that every future reader must understand. Set a flag removal deadline when the flag is created.
Delete flag check; make new behaviour unconditional
Delete flag and all guarded code
Remove flag; hardcode new model version

# Flag cleanup checklist — run before removing a flag

def remove_prompt_flag(flag_name: str, flag_file: str = "feature_flags.py"):
    """Steps to safely remove a feature flag after GA."""
    steps = [
        f"1. Search codebase for all references to {flag_name}",
        "2. Verify flag is in GA state (rollout = 100% or flag enabled for all users)",
        "3. Remove flag check from get_system_prompt() — make the guarded behaviour unconditional",
        "4. Remove flag field from FeatureFlagContext dataclass",
        "5. Remove env var read from read_flags()",
        "6. Remove unit tests that test flag-off behaviour (the feature is now always on)",
        "7. Update tests that test flag-on behaviour — remove the flag setup, keep the assertion",
        f"8. Delete env var {flag_name} from all deployment configs and CI yml files",
        "9. Deploy to staging; verify feature still works (no env var needed now)",
        "10. Open PR titled: chore: remove flag {flag_name} after GA",
    ]
    for step in steps:
        print(step)
User A sees new feature; User A on retry sees old feature
Use deterministic rollout bucket (md5(user_id) % 100), not random()
Flag not evaluated at request time
Operator changes env var; users still see old behaviour
Read os.environ inside read_flags() on each request, not at module load time
Kill switch enabled but AI still running (other flag enabled it)
Always check disable flag before any other flag evaluation
Tests interfere with each other; flag state leaks between test cases
Inject FeatureFlagContext as parameter; never read os.environ in the function under test
Users who had the feature on suddenly lose it
Remove flag only after rollout_pct = 100 and SLO stable for ≥ 24h
Operational Runbook: Responding to a Canary Problem
When a canary shows a metric anomaly (error rate spike, latency increase, escalation rate spike), the response sequence is:
: set FF_ESCALATION_V2=false (or the relevant flag) in the production config. This takes effect within one request cycle without a deploy.
: confirm the metric anomaly stops within 5 minutes of the flag being disabled.
: before touching any code, save the flag state, the failing request IDs (from logs), and the metric charts.
: reproduce the failure in the eval suite using the captured request examples. Add them as new eval cases so the same failure can never pass CI again.
: fix the prompt or tool change, run the updated eval suite, restart the rollout from Internal Testing.

# Emergency flag disable (no deploy needed)
# Option 1: update environment variable in your config service
aws ssm put-parameter --name "/prod/FF_ESCALATION_V2" --value "false" --overwrite

# Option 2: if flags are in a config file that is hot-reloaded
echo "FF_ESCALATION_V2=false" >> /etc/app/feature_flags.env

# Option 3: if using a feature flag service (e.g. LaunchDarkly, Flagsmith)
# Use the dashboard to toggle the flag off — takes effect immediately

# Verify the flag is off within one request cycle
curl -s https://api.internal/health/flags | jq .FF_ESCALATION_V2
# Should return: false

Feature flags for AI features decouple deployment from activation, keeping trunk stable: a prompt change can be on main and deployable before any user sees it. The flag lifecycle has four states: Merged (flag off), Internal Testing, Canary (1-5% rollout by deterministic user bucket), and General Availability. An Eval Gate (all must-do and must-not-do eval cases passing) controls the Internal→Canary transition; an SLO Gate (error rate, latency p99, escalation rate stable for ≥24 hours) controls the Canary→GA transition. A kill switch flag is always the outermost check, allowing instant disable without a deploy. Flags should be injected as a dataclass parameter rather than read inside functions, so tests can set any flag state independently. Flags are removed within one sprint of GA to prevent technical debt accumulation.
